extra.message: User input from request.args.get('search') is interpolated directly into an SQL query via f-string on line 137 and executed on line 138. The 'search_query' variable is not sanitized or parameterized, allowing SQL injection through the dashboard search feature (e.g., ?search=' OR 1=1--).

User-supplied 'picture_url' from the JSON request body is passed directly to requests.get() without any URL validation or allowlist filtering. An attacker can provide internal URLs (e.g., http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/, http://localhost:5000/) to perform Server-Side Request Forgery, accessing internal services and cloud metadata endpoints.

The DELETE /api/v1/delete-user/<user_id> endpoint has no authentication or authorization checks. The @token_required decorator is commented out (line 221-222). Any unauthenticated user can delete arbitrary user accounts by sending a DELETE request with any user_id, leading to complete account takeover/destruction.

cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

extra.message: The GET /api/v1/profile endpoint has no authentication check. The @token_required decorator is commented out (line 152-153). Any unauthenticated user can retrieve any user's profile information (username, email, country, role, permissions, team) by providing a user_id parameter, leading to mass data exposure via IDOR.

The 'search_query' variable (sourced from request.args.get('search') in routes.py:126) is rendered with the '| safe' Jinja2 filter in the search input's value attribute. The '| safe' filter disables auto-escaping, allowing reflected XSS. An attacker can craft a URL like /dashboard?search="><script>alert(1)</script> to execute arbitrary JavaScript.

The user.country field is rendered with the '| safe' Jinja2 filter, disabling auto-escaping. Since the country field is user-controlled (set during signup or profile update via routes.py:186), an attacker can store malicious JavaScript in the country field (e.g., <script>alert(1)</script>) which will execute when any user views the profile page.

User-supplied comment input is directly appended to the DOM using jQuery's .append('
  • ' + comment + '
    • ') without any HTML encoding or sanitization. An attacker can enter HTML/JavaScript in the comment input field (e.g., ) to execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of any user viewing the page.

The /update_profile endpoint accepts 'role' and 'permissions' fields from user-supplied form data (lines 187-196). Although the HTML form has these fields commented out, an attacker can add hidden form fields or craft a POST request with 'role=admin' and 'permissions=all' to escalate their privileges to admin, gaining full administrative access.

cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

extra.message: The Flask SECRET_KEY is hardcoded as 'just_another_secret'. This key is used for signing session cookies and JWT tokens. An attacker who knows this key can forge session cookies to impersonate any user and forge JWT tokens to bypass API authentication, effectively achieving complete authentication bypass.

Passwords are hashed using MD5 (hashlib.md5) without any salt. MD5 is cryptographically broken and unsuitable for password storage. Attackers can use rainbow tables or brute force to reverse MD5 hashes extremely quickly, compromising all user passwords in the database.

The uploaded file's original filename (file.filename) is used directly in os.path.join() without sanitization (e.g., werkzeug.utils.secure_filename). An attacker can upload a file with a crafted filename like '../../../etc/cron.d/malicious' to write files to arbitrary locations on the server filesystem via path traversal.

SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY is set to False, allowing JavaScript to access the session cookie via document.cookie. Combined with the XSS vulnerabilities present in this application, this enables session hijacking where an attacker can steal session tokens through malicious JavaScript.

Flask DEBUG mode is hardcoded to True. In production, debug mode exposes the Werkzeug interactive debugger which allows remote code execution if the debugger PIN is known or bypassed. It also exposes detailed stack traces with source code and local variables to users, leaking sensitive information.

cwe: CWE-215: Insertion of Sensitive Information Into Debugging Code

extra.message: The POST /api/v1/update_picture endpoint has no authentication or authorization. Any unauthenticated user can update any user's profile picture by providing an arbitrary username in the JSON body. Combined with the SSRF vulnerability, this allows an attacker to both trigger SSRF and modify other users' profile pictures.

cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

extra.message: The /upload endpoint does not validate file type, extension, or content. There is no check on the file extension or MIME type, allowing upload of any file type including executable scripts (.py, .sh, .php), HTML files with embedded JavaScript, or other malicious payloads. These files are served back via /uploads/<filename> which could lead to stored XSS or remote code execution depending on server configuration.

cwe: CWE-434: Unrestricted Upload of File with Dangerous Type

extra.message: Exception details are printed to the server console with print('Error inserting user:', e). While not directly exposed to end users through the response, in debug mode (DEBUG=True) with the Werkzeug debugger, exception details including database error messages could be exposed, potentially revealing database schema information.

cwe: CWE-209: Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information

extra.message: The /api/v1/update_picture endpoint returns raw exception messages to the user via str(e) in the 500 response on line 298. This can leak internal server details, file paths, network configuration, or database information to attackers, aiding further exploitation.

cwe: CWE-209: Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information

extra.message: JWT tokens are signed using the hardcoded SECRET_KEY 'just_another_secret' (from config.py:7). This allows an attacker to forge valid JWT tokens for any user by signing them with the known secret, completely bypassing API authentication on any endpoint protected by @token_required.

The /download/<filename> route passes the user-controlled 'filename' parameter to send_from_directory(). While send_from_directory provides some path traversal protection, the filename is also used in a database query. If an attacker uploaded a file with a traversal name (via the unvalidated upload), they can download it from the server. The same issue applies to /uploads/<filename> route (line 283) and /delete/<filename> route (line 340) where filename is used with os.path.join and os.remove without sanitization.

The /delete/<filename> route constructs a file path using os.path.join(UPLOAD_FOLDER, filename) with unsanitized user input at line 342, then deletes it with os.remove(file_path) at line 364. Although there is a database ownership check, if a file was uploaded with a path-traversal filename (possible via the unvalidated upload), it can be used to delete arbitrary files from the filesystem.
